meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a motion decision is right or wrong.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: When animation quality is poor, the first fix should be removing motion, not adding more.
logic: Almost all low-quality animation is over-animated, not under-animated.
Removing a motion group reduces cognitive load on the viewer and technical load on the engine.
Adding effects to fix quality gives the agent more moving parts to tune, which usually makes the problem worse.
After removal, what remains is the essential communication — which can then be timed and eased intentionally.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before adjusting any easing curve or duration, delete one motion group and see if the scene is clearer. Repeat until only essential motion remains. Then polish.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Every quality problem can be traced to a structural cause: attention, timing, density, easing, or performance.
logic: 'Cheap' usually means high density + linear or mismatched easing + no holds.
'AI-looking' usually means uniform timing + uniform easing + continuous motion without pauses.
'Chaotic' usually means unsequenced motion groups + competing attention + no primary subject.
Each label has a diagnostic checklist. Run the checklist before offering a remedy.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Replace every taste label with a structural diagnosis before acting. 'This feels cheap' becomes '5 simultaneous motion groups use mixed easings with no holds.' Then fix that.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: The only valid benchmark for animation quality is whether the motion helps or hinders the viewer's primary task. Animation that is beautiful but competes with reading has failed.
logic: A viewer comes to a page or video with a task: read, learn, decide, compare, navigate, enjoy.
Motion that supports the task (guides attention, clarifies hierarchy, provides feedback) is good motion.
Motion that competes with the task (distracts during reading, hides the CTA, confuses the hierarchy) is bad motion — regardless of its aesthetic quality.
The most common post-launch animation failure: the parallax/drift/particle animation makes the page 'feel alive' but reduces conversion by competing with the CTA.
reasoning_chains.so_what: For every review, state the viewer's primary task in one sentence. Then ask: 'Does this animation help or hinder that task?' Be specific about the mechanism. 'The floating particles compete with headline reading' is actionable. 'The animation feels off' is not.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Easing character is the most underdiagnosed cause of animation feeling wrong. It is not a technical setting — it is the emotional tone of movement.
logic: Bounce easing on a cybersecurity product = the brand reads as unreliable. The motion says 'fun toy' while the brand says 'trusted security.'
Linear easing on a luxury brand = the motion reads as mechanical and cheap. Luxury needs acceleration curves that suggest handcrafted quality.
Power-in easing (fast start, slow stop) on a CTA = the button seems to hit an invisible wall and stop abruptly. The user hesitates.
The wrong easing can make a technically perfect animation fail at communication, and most reviewers blame the animation itself rather than the easing choice.
reasoning_chains.so_what: In every review, check: does the easing character match the brand tone? Does each element's easing tell the right story? Bounce = playful, consumer, casual. Power-out = confident, premium, deliberate. Power-in = heavy, loading, arrival. Elastic = exaggerated, comic, attention-getting. If uncertain, default to power2.out or power3.out — they read as neutral-to-confident and rarely undermine the message.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Density is the fastest diagnostic. Count simultaneous motion groups. If above 3, the review can stop there — there is a density problem, and no amount of timing polish will fix it.
logic: Motion density (simultaneous animated groups) is the single strongest predictor of perceived quality.
Below 3 groups: the viewer can track everything. Quality differences come from timing and easing.
3-5 groups: the viewer starts to feel 'busy.' Specific elements get lost.
5+ groups: the viewer perceives chaos. No individual element registers. The animation communicates nothing.
Fixing density must come before fixing anything else. Polishing easing on a 7-group scene is polishing a broken structure.
reasoning_chains.so_what: First diagnostic step in every review: count the maximum number of simultaneous motion groups. If >3, do not discuss timing, easing, or effects. The only valid feedback is 'reduce simultaneous motion groups to 3 or fewer.' After density is fixed, then review timing and easing.
